data.title: Amazon: Just Like Google In 2023
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock has failed to impress despite AWS (Amazon Web Services) accelerating over 400 basis points sequentially in Q4 FY25, while its custom silicon, specifically Trainium and Graviton, now has a combined annual revenue run rate exceeding $10B.

In fact, just a few weeks after the company’s Q4 earnings, OpenAI announced an $110B funding round where Amazon would be investing $50B in a multi-year strategic partnership with the company. As part of this deal, OpenAI committed 2 gigawatts of Trainium capacity while expanding the original $38B AWS compute agreement from November by $100B over eight years, directly adding to AWS’s top line.

This news too failed to move the needle on the stock, as investors remain squarely focused on the question of Amazon’s capex sustainability.

In the company’s latest earnings, Amazon management raised their FY26 capex guidance by over 50% to $200B, which might lead to their free cash flow turning negative, as the company would now have to tap into the debt markets to fund its AI buildout ambitions.

The way I see it, Amazon looks quite similar to Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) in early 2023. In 2023, investors were heavily skeptical that Google might face extensive disruption to its search monopoly from OpenAI’s ChatGPT. Yet, no one was paying attention to the work Google was quietly doing to control the entire AI value chain with its custom TPUs and utilizing them to build Gemini and optimizing the performance of its services (Search, YouTube, etc.).

Amazon today is in a similar spot, where it is fiercely doubling down on its custom silicon roadmap, which in turn is helping it accelerate its AWS revenue growth as enterprises look to diversify away from Nvidia’s (NASDAQ:NVDA) GPUs, while Bedrock creates a powerful ecosystem lock-in in the AWS stack.

Unfortunately, investors are looking past it all and may only be convinced when they see a bottoming in the company’s free cash flow to re-rate the stock higher. At the moment, long-term investors, such as myself, need to remain patient with this company, as the risk-reward continues to remain attractive.

Fierce Custom Silicon Strategy Keeps AWS Acceleration Intact

Amazon’s AWS revenue accelerated yet again in Q4 FY25, growing 24% YoY, and it does not look like it will stop anytime soon.

During the quarter, AWS revenue reached an annualized run rate of $142B, with their custom silicon business, including Graviton (custom CPU) and Trainium (custom XPU/accelerator), at a $10B annual revenue run rate, growing triple-digit percentages year-over-year, and backlog reaching $244B, representing a year-on-year increase of 40%.

A few weeks after Amazon reported its earnings, OpenAI announced an $110B funding round, with Amazon investing $50B in a multi-year strategic partnership with the company.

Note this is not an extension of the November deal, where OpenAI had committed $38B over seven years to rent NVIDIA GPU capacity on AWS. In the latest strategic partnership between the two companies, Amazon has skin in the game, unlike before, with its $50B investment, which will be broken into two chunks consisting of $15B upfront, followed by $35B conditional on certain requirements being met.

Not only that, while the November deal was explicitly about renting Nvidia capacity on AWS, the latest deal has OpenAI committing 2 gigawatts of Trainium capacity, which is a strong validation of Amazon’s custom silicon strategy, along with AWS becoming the exclusive third-party cloud distribution provider for OpenAI’s enterprise agent platform called Frontier.

Finally, the latest deal also expands the original $38B AWS compute agreement by $100B over eight years, directly adding to AWS’s top line, as OpenAI boosts its forward revenue target to $280B by 2030, up from $20B in 2025, a feat that would require at least $600B in infrastructure spending on OpenAI’s part.

What really strikes me is Amazon’s fierce push of its custom silicon in order to set itself apart. During the earnings call, Trainium (which is their custom accelerator) was mentioned 27 times, while Nvidia was not mentioned at all. This is what management said below that demonstrates the nature of the force in which they are ramping up their Trainium2 chips while preparing for the future generations of custom accelerators.

“We've landed over 1.4 million Trainium2 chips, our fastest ramping chip launch ever. Trainium2 is 30% to 40% more price-performant than comparable GPUs and is a multibillion-dollar annualized revenue run rate business with 100,000-plus companies using it, as Trainium is the majority underpinning of Bedrock usage today. We recently launched Trainium3, which is up to 40% more price-performant than Trainium2. We're seeing very strong demand for Trainium3, and expect nearly all of our Trainium3 supply of chips to be committed by mid-2026. And though we're still building Trainium4, we're seeing very strong interest already.”

The same is true for their custom CPU silicon, Graviton, which management mentioned is up to 40% more price performant than leading x86 processors and is used by 90% of top AWS’s 1000 customers.

In fact, Anthropic has already been training its latest Claude model on Trainium2 chips in Amazon’s Project Rainier cluster, which is one of the primary reasons behind the reacceleration in AWS revenue, which I have discussed in my previous posts.

With the latest commitment from OpenAI to consume 2GW of Trainium capacity along with $100B worth of compute spend on AWS, Goldman expects that the two AI labs could generate a combined AWS revenue of $13.2B in 2026, which could potentially reach $60.1B by 2029, accounting for over 20% of total AWS revenue.

The way I see it, Amazon’s fierce push of its custom silicon roadmap is starting to look a lot like Google’s vertical integration strategy, where the company owns its full hardware and software stack with its TPUs (Tensor Processing Units) that it built to optimize its own services such as Search, YouTube, etc., while bypassing the “Nvidia tax” to drive superior price-performance. While Google originally reserved TPUs for internal projects, they are now widely used by external companies and are one of the primary reasons why Google Cloud has been accelerating higher, as enterprises seek lower-cost alternatives to Nvidia’s GPUs.

We are now starting to see a similar pattern play out for Amazon AWS, as the company is rapidly accelerating their custom silicon release cadences with Trainium3 and Trainium4 while offering an end-to-end platform that is tied to the AWS ecosystem.

Capex Altering Free Cash Flow Dynamics Concerns Investors

Yet, despite the acceleration in AWS, a trend that I do not expect to slow for the next several quarters, investors remain concerned.

In fact, the stock has barely moved over the last year, despite its revenue and earnings per share growing 12.38% and 28.84%, respectively.

This is similar to Google in the first part of 2023, when the stock struggled relative to other hyperscalers as investors feared that Google’s search monopoly would be disrupted by ChatGPT, especially when the company announced Code Red along with several regulatory headwinds that it was facing at that time.

In the case of Amazon, investor skepticism is tied to the company’s full-year capex guidance, which is expected to grow over 50% YoY to $200B in FY26. However, it is not just Amazon that has massively raised its full-year capex guidance, but also Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT), Meta (NASDAQ:META) and Google, which are now looking to invest roughly $680B in AI capex for 2026 (including Amazon), roughly double that of the FY25 figure.

The reason why such an elevated level of capex is concerning is because it alters the free cash flow mechanics. In the case of Amazon, its free cash flow is expected to turn negative this year, as its operating cash flow will not grow fast enough to cover the planned $200B in capex in FY26. This would mean that the AI buildout, which was once funded by self-sustaining operating cash flows, will now require Amazon tapping into the debt markets, like it did recently with its largest corporate bond offering, raising approximately $54B earlier this month.

Yet, the encouraging sign is the backlog at Amazon has been growing at an accelerated rate over the last two quarters, with CEO Andy Jassy reiterating that they are "monetizing capacity as fast as we can install it," suggesting that the conversion of the backlog into revenue is accelerating as they add capacity.

What is also important to note is that investors calculating return on capex utilizing current revenue estimates is fundamentally flawed. As Rihard Jarc from New Era Funds points out, the capex a hyperscaler spends on a data center in a given year will be utilized over a 2-year period, as it takes around 2 years to build and operationalize a data center.

Therefore, when we are looking at 2025 revenue growth for hyperscalers, in this case, Amazon, we should translate that into capex spent in 2023, not in 2024 or 2025. The same is true for 2026 capex, which will translate into revenue two years down the line. At the moment, consensus estimates are pointing to revenue upwards of $1T in FY28, which is being revised higher, as can be seen below.

Meanwhile, Nvidia’s CEO Jensen Huang also firmly defended the $3-4T of AI capex buildout over the next several years, as I discussed in my Oracle post last week. According to him, the AI buildout is necessary in order to handle the exponential growth in tokenization as we enter the Agentic AI era.

“In this new world of AI, compute equals revenues. Without compute, there’s no way to generate tokens. Without tokens, there’s no way to grow revenues. [...] I am certain at this point that we’ve reached the inflection point.”

Valuation & Conclusions

Amazon is currently trading at a non-GAAP forward PE ratio of 27.5. When we take FY28 earnings per share expectations into account, the forward PE ratio compresses to under 18, which is slightly below the 10-year average of the S&P 500.

As I explained below, investors are heavily skeptical of the altering free cash flow dynamics from the surging capex while also discounting the risk of failed or delayed revenue realization from the backlog, which would further strain margins and free cash flow.

The way I see it, until investors see a bottoming in free cash flow margins, chances are that investor pessimism may linger, despite AWS likely accelerating its top line growth in the coming quarters.

On a technical basis, if the stock is able to close above its 100 DMA at $227, it would likely head higher towards $240 per share. Whether it reaches an all-time high in the near term is difficult to say at the moment amid heightened scrutiny on AI capex as well as geopolitical uncertainty that has been plaguing the S&P 500.

What is encouraging is that Wall Street’s average price target of $280 per share suggests a 33% upside, compared to the downside risk of 16% to the lowest price target of $175 per share, as can be seen below.

As a result, I will continue to hold my position in Amazon at current levels but might trim into strength to reduce the size of my exposure in it depending on the evolving market narrative at the time.

Note that aside from their AWS business, Amazon derives 82% of its revenue from its North America and International Segments, which are heavily subject to macroeconomic volatility. Plus, it remains to be seen how Amazon manages resource allocation and its custom silicon roadmap now that it holds strategic investments in two competing AI labs, which is a great hedge on paper but could be a lot more nuanced in practice.

data.title: Hudson Pacific: Why You Have To Look Past The 6X FFO Multiple
data.description:
In our previous coverage of Hudson Pacific Properties (HPP) we explained why the common shares were not yet on the pink sheets. Our conclusion did not have any cheers for the long-suffering bulls, and this time we extended our warnings up the capital structure.

This is a fairly unusual situation where management has shown fortitude to dilute this late in the game. But we don't think this saves the day. The headwinds are too big. At this point, we are maintaining our Strong Sell ratings on the stock and the preferred. We are also suggesting a full-scale "abandon ship" on the bonds. They were priced for distress, but despite the equity raise, we don't see them as attractive.

Source: Something Happened On The Way To Heaven

The common shares have headed in the right direction,

but the Hudson Pacific Properties, Inc. 4.750% CUM PFD C (HPP.PR.C) have been more resilient (down just 12%). We review the recently released Q4-2025 and update our outlook.

Q4-2025

HPP's early data slide from their presentation gives everyone 80% of the information they need to abandon ship. We show the slide below with highlighted portions and discuss them next.

The key takeaways there are,

1) Office occupancy rates are still falling.

2) While more leases were executed this quarter, the rents are really, really low ($41.41 a square foot).

3) Studio occupancy looks horrid. Some might blame this on the LLM mania, but we think that factor has nothing to do with it.

4) Adjusted funds from operations were negative 14 cents, despite a massive share count increase from last year.

5) Debt to EBITDA looks a bit better than last year, thanks to some asset sales and the equity raise. But the 11.2X is still so far into the crimson tide, that no one is sleeping easy.

6) The weighted average debt maturity is a tiny 2.6 years.

The key context on the rents we mentioned can be seen below. Most of HPP's maturing rents are so far below that $41.41 that it will literally go under even if it improves its occupancy levels while letting rents drop. We have highlighted a few of the quarters and years below, and 2028 is the final deadline for this company.

To be fair, HPP has some great assets. The recently completed Washington 1000 is a prime example of a trophy asset. Well, about as "trophy" as any office asset can be in today's market.

HPP just finished building this, and based on their renting site, the entire building is vacant.

Ok, almost the entire building is vacant.

We will note that the building was completed more than a year back, and there are just no bids for spaces. We are sure HPP management was hoping for a strong showing from this asset to help them out.

Outlook

Management had a positive outlook.

Following our significant derisking in 2025, our priorities are clear and executable: drive occupancy growth to unlock embedded NOI expansion, eliminate Quixote's earnings drag by year-end, and maintain capital discipline through value-driven assets, sales and strategic de-leveraging. On our capital recycling, we sold Element LA in the fourth quarter at a strong valuation. In 2026, we're targeting $200 million to $300 million of additional sales, while prioritizing transactions that are FFO-accretive through further de-leveraging.

For example, we're currently marketing 10900 and 10950 Washington in Culver City, which we successfully re-entitled for 508 residential units, and we have a very strong buyer and joint venture interest throughout.

Source: HPP Q4-2025 Conference Call Transcript

They are also forecasting a solid FFO number for the year and rising occupancies.

Turning to our 2026 outlook. Due to the progress Victor and Mark described, we are reinstating full year FFO guidance at $0.96 to $1.06 per diluted share. We anticipate slightly lower FFO in the first quarter relative to the fourth quarter 2025, followed by steady sequential growth throughout the year as our leasing pipeline converts to cash flow.

We are introducing annual average in-service office occupancy guidance of 80% to 82%. Clearly, our year-end occupancy will exceed this range.

Source: HPP Q4-2025 Conference Call Transcript

This will create a big turnaround from the net operating income drop we saw in 2025.

But the elephant in the room is still the debt. The $2.7 billion of debt (at HPP's share) is about 8 times the current common equity market capitalization. That maturity breakdown is also about as ominous as it gets.

You will note points number 4 and 7 below as well.

Management was pressed on this, but we did not get a definitive answer on that massive CMBS pool up for refinancing.

Blaine Heck Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division

Great. That's all very helpful. Just a couple of questions on the upcoming CMBS maturity on the Hollywood Media Portfolio. Can you just talk about kind of the tenor of those conversations with the lenders. Do you see an extension as a potential outcome? And would you expect to need an equity infusion of any sort for the refinancing or potential extension there?

Victor Coleman Chairman & CEO

So Blaine, we're unable to just discuss specifics over our loan extension negotiations because currently today, there's ongoing dialogue, and we're focused on the best outcome for us and our shareholders, including the respect of capital allocation. So we're not going to get into an open dialogue on a conference call as to the status, but we're in constant communication, and we're happy with the progress so far.

Source: HPP Q4-2025 Conference Call Transcript

Our sense is that this will require a 50% paydown. Half of that could come from select asset sales and the other half from yet another massive equity raise. And if they do all that, the first video game wave of the bad guys (read debt maturities) will be dealt with. Every single year is like that till 2029.

So you can sense our hesitation in trying to bottom-fish this.

From our first sell rating, the stock is down nearly 90%.

That is despite a rather unanticipated equity offering late in the game. A major real estate player took a big share of that, leading many investors to ask, "What are they seeing that the bears are missing?" Unfortunately, arguments from authority almost never work, and their investment is down 62% since then.

Even if the equity survives, we think the dilution required will create an effective wipeout. Effective wipeout here means another 90% price drop, adjusted, of course, for the inevitable stock splits. Just refinancing the entire capital stack at 7% rates means a wipeout. HPP bonds currently yield between 8% and 11%.

Those getting excited at the "6X FFO" should know that translates into $65 million of cash flow. $65 million won't even make a dent in the $2.7 billion of debt. So we are going to maintain the ratings with the caveat that the stock is very oversold, and a rebound to $8 could certainly happen on its way to $1.00.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it sounds like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Europe Can Regain Its Productivity Edge By Scaling Up
data.description:
By Diego Cerdeiro, deputy unit chief in the Regional Studies unit of the IMF’s European department | Stephan Danninger, Head, IMF’s Regional Studies Unit for Europe | Morgan Maneely, Senior Research Officer in the Advanced Economies unit of the IMF’s European Department

Capital markets integration, expanding opportunities for workers, and bigger consumer markets will allow companies to grow faster

Europe once led the world in productivity growth but now lags the United States — and the gap has widened significantly in recent years.

The Chart of the Week shows that behind this shortfall is the staggering difficulty that European firms face in scaling up. In the United States, the stock market valuation of young firms (under the age of 50) is $42.9 trillion, compared to a meager $5 trillion in the European Union.

This reflects imperfections in European integration. For all the achievements of the European single market, capital flows remain fragmented along national lines, opportunities for workers are hampered by regulations, and it is often difficult to market products across borders. The result is that the EU has too many small, old, and low-growth companies. The average European firm that has been in business for 25 years or more employs about 10 workers. A comparable US company employs 70 people.

It is therefore no surprise that Europe’s labor productivity levels are about 20 percent below those of the United States.

Our research shows that addressing this issue requires integration at various levels: of capital markets, to allow more funding to go to risky new businesses; of labor markets, to allow people to move to opportunity; and of consumer markets, so that companies can sell to bigger markets. The good news is that these are all changes that Europe can bring about.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Deja Vu--Spiking Oil Prices, Surging Credit Worries
data.description:
Events in Iran have dominated headlines over the past two weeks. Anyone watching the minute-by-minute movement in the oil markets over the past several days probably has a case of motion sickness given the extreme gyrations. The Strait of Hormuz has effectively been closed since the start of this conflict. This has locked in approximately 20% of global crude exports and is having a bigger impact than either Covid or the Ukrainian invasion early in 2022.

It is hard to model when an 'off ramp' to this expanding war will develop or what it will look like. Given this, I thought it would be a good idea to explore what an extended oil price shock could have on the global economy and the markets in my column today. In addition, the events in the Middle East are not happening in a vacuum. Every few days or so another risk emerges from the credit markets, which I recently covered in this article.

First, the good news. The U.S. is substantially more energy-independent than Europe and especially Asia, which gets roughly 60% of its energy imports from the Middle East as does India. Second, both the U.S. and the global economy are much less dependent on oil than they were in the 1970s during the OPEC oil embargos.

Oil intensity has dropped dramatically over that time. This metric measures the amount of oil required to produce one unit of GDP and it has declined by more than half since the 1970s as can be seen above. In addition, Europe plans to release 400 million barrels of oil from its reserves. The U.S. is going to let 172 million barrels of oil out of its Strategic Petroleum Reserves as well.

Now, for the litany of bad news. Just over 20 million barrels of oil transits through the Strait of Hormuz daily, so the reserves the U.S. and Europe are releasing will be eaten up by global demand quickly. And with tankers now being attacked regularly, it is hard to see how this passage reopens until the overall conflict is resolved. In addition, roughly half of the available LNG carriers are trapped in the Persian Gulf as well.

And it is not just oil and natural gas being impacted. As can be seen above, a whole slew of chemicals and metals are also being blocked in by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, triggering rallies in those prices. Most notably, nitrogen and phosphate, two key ingredients for fertilizer. This could ignite higher food inflation in coming months.

In addition, while the SPR can hold up to 714 million barrels of oil, it currently holds just over 410 million barrels. The reserve was drained by approximately 200 million barrels during the summer of 2022 as gasoline prices spiked and in front of the November mid-terms that year. These reserves have not been replaced since then.

The problem with an asymmetric conflict is the inferior side as far as military force goes, doesn't have to win. They just have to endure. For Iran, that means keeping the current regime roughly intact, preventing all or most of the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz and retaining some capability to hit U.S. forces, Israel and assets in other Gulf countries. And outside of 'boots on the ground', it seems the country will likely be able to endure in the short to medium term at a minimum. And my view is neither the American public nor Congress will go along with marching into Iran with the U.S. infantry.

This means this conflict is likely to continue. Oil will continue to rise and remain far above $100/barrel with severe impacts to the U.S. and global economy. And historically, the last few times that oil has had huge sustainable spikes, it has not played out well for either equities or the U.S. economy. A $10/barrel rise in oil prices, roughly equates to a .2% increase in the CPI. This likely takes any additional cuts to the Fed Funds rate until this situation is resolved.

Us old duffers remember the gas lines, stagflation, and general misery that followed the OPEC embargos of 1973 and 1979. However, many forget there was a large rise in crude prices just before the Great Financial Crisis as well. This coincided with the subprime crisis, which was the primary trigger of that economic debacle. And with new 'cockroaches' emerging in the credit markets in what feels like a daily basis now, will history repeat?

Events, if they continue to escalate in the Middle East, could trigger a further rally in hard assets. During the 1973 OPEC embargo, gold doubled from $90 to $180/oz in a year. In the 1979 OPEC event, gold prices went from $220 to $850 an ounce in a year.

The so-called HALO trade, 'heavy asset, low obsolescence', is gaining some currency on the Street. The thesis being 'companies with substantial physical capital and durable economic relevance will outperform asset-light businesses like software makers'. In essence, invest in businesses and sectors that AI will be hard pressed to duplicate or replace to any significant degree.

Market valuations are at extreme levels based on historical metrics, and chances of a resolution in the Middle East are unlikely in the days ahead. Therefore, my portfolio continues to be conservatively allocated, split roughly 75% in covered call positions within the few stocks in this market that sport reasonable valuations. Most of these are within sectors like healthcare, consumer staples and energy; that should do better than the overall market if oil prices continue to rise. The rest of my portfolio is largely in cash and short-term treasuries, awaiting lower entry points that seem likely if this conflict continues.

data.title: Pharma's Godfather Moment: Pulled Back Into Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
By Diederik Stadig, Senior Economist, Healthcare & Technology

'Just when I thought I was out...'

Just as 16 branded pharma companies struck investment deals with the Administration – trading MFN pricing for a temporary reprieve on tariffs – the US Supreme Court ruling marked the start of a messy transition period. In response, the Administration invoked Section 122 of the 1974 Trade Act, which permits tariffs of up to 15% for as long as 150 days, with the possibility of extensions.

Crucially, pharmaceuticals fall under enumerated exceptions, meaning the tariff does not apply to them. Yet the renewed uncertainty feels familiar. As Michael Corleone puts it in The Godfather Part III: “Just when I thought I was out, they pull me back in.”

We expect MFN deals to hold

In spite of the messy transition period, we expect MFN deals to hold. This means that more investment in US manufacturing will happen, but it may be delayed until there is legal certainty surrounding tariffs. Furthermore, the US government is unlikely to renege on its commitments to the branded pharma space, because TrumpRx and MFN have been touted by the Administration.

In addition, the sector is increasingly seen as integral to national security, which means there is a significant incentive to keep manufacturing and innovation close to home.

Tariff on generics will remain zero

For the generic sector, where the supply chain mostly sits in Asia, we expect the tariff rate to remain zero. It would be political suicide to levy tariffs on generic pharmaceuticals because the supply chain will not relocate as a result of the tariff threat, which would mean higher drug prices. And since 91% of all US prescription drugs are generics, these effects would be felt quickly. Moreover, this Administration has committed itself to lower drug prices.

The US imports more than half of its pharmaceuticals from China

CDMOs are beneficiaries

As we have written about before, the net beneficiaries of these uncertain times are CDMOs, who offer a way out of uncertainty. As companies can use CDMO capacity to localise their manufacturing, this is why we expect high single-digit CDMO growth until 2030.

Global CDMO market to experience strong growth until 2030

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: Sweetgreen: Wraps For The Win? I Doubt It
data.description:
2025 was a rough year for Sweetgreen, Inc. (SG) as the macro pressures coupled with poor execution resulted in the company’s stock price falling from over $30 a share to now single digits. So far, 2026 isn’t faring much better, as the stock is down over 16% after a disappointing 2026 guide.

Let’s get into the details of the company’s latest financial results as well as business metrics as I share why I’m becoming more skeptical of this business.

Plans and Projects for 2026

Let’s start by discussing traffic, which was down 13.3% in the fourth quarter despite a 1.8% benefit from menu price increases. The company’s same-store sales also clearly illustrate that Sweetgreen is struggling to get customers in the door, as you can see below that same stores declined by over 11% in the quarter and declined by roughly 8% for the year:

As I noted in my prior article, Sweetgreen has relied on Millennials and Gen-Z consumers to frequently visit, and that hasn’t been happening, as the data from Black Box Intelligence stated younger adults are feeling financial stress, which led to these consumers cutting back.

Sweetgreen is hoping their "Project One Best Way" will help improve operational efficiencies. On the company’s earnings call, Sweetgreen’s CEO and President, Jonathan Neman, had this to say about Project One Best Way: “Over the summer, we implemented Project One Best Way, our system-wide effort to elevate operational excellence through clear standards, performance-based leadership, and measured execution. Today, approximately 2/3 of our restaurants are hitting our great bar based on our internal operational audit. What's most encouraging is the shift in the distribution this quarter, with more restaurants exceeding standard and fewer falling below, reflecting improved consistency across the fleet.”

I’m not sure what management means by “great,” as the results since this project began have been anything but that, so I’m skeptical these improvements are meaningfully impacting the business.

The company is now implementing another plan called the “Sweet Growth Transformation Plan.” This is a five-part plan that includes the following strategic priorities: 1. Operational Excellence (which I’d say is essential) is “Project One Best Way,” 2. Food Quality and Menu Innovation, 3. Personalized Experience, 4. Brand Relevance, and 5. Disciplined Profitable Investment.

I’d say food quality and menu innovation are likely the strategic priorities Sweetgreen must nail if they want to turn things around this year. On the earnings call, Neman noted the business had a promotional $10 'Tis the Season Harvest Bowl, which seemed to go well, so the company is working on testing a “Create Your Own” platform, which will give consumers options, and they will also be more transparent about price.

Neman also noted the business is launching wraps. Sweetgreen is launching wraps like the Classic Chicken Caesar and the Chicken Jalapeno Ranch, all of which have a price point below $15.

While I'm somewhat optimistic about the company’s bowl concepts like “Create Your Own,” I’m far from sold on the wrap idea. This seems to be playing to market themes, and in a macro environment where the consumer is struggling, I don’t see a world where they’d go to a Sweetgreen for an $11 wrap rather than go to McDonald's (MCD), where you could get two or three wraps for the same price. McDonald's is also just one example, as numerous restaurants like Burger King and Popeye's also recently launched new wraps.

To me, Sweetgreen is not going to win back consumers or differentiate themselves with wraps.

Risks

I like reviewing a company’s 10-K filing to see what the company views as significant risks for the business. Two obvious ones that I won’t get into are, competition, as there are numerous options when it comes to restaurants, and the previously mentioned overall macro picture.

One significant risk Sweetgreen noted in their Risk Factors section of the 10-K is, “We may not persuade customers of the benefits of paying our prices for higher-quality food.” To me, that’s central to Sweetgreen’s investment theme, as younger generations were willing to pay up for these higher-quality ingredients, but it seems the tide has turned. I don’t think the business is doing enough with their offerings to justify higher prices. Customers have even been pushing back on companies like Chipotle (CMG) and Cava (CAVA), which I’d argue are healthy options with more unique menu offerings.

There is also some concentration risk, as about one-fourth of the company’s revenue has been coming from New York. Restaurants in the New York metro area accounted for 24%, 25%, and 28% over the last three fiscal years. Sweetgreen is planning to expand to Salt Lake City and Nashville in 2026, so I imagine this percentage will continue to drop. However, this does also have me concerned that Sweetgreen may only work in larger and trendier metros (which have younger populations as well) compared to, say, Chipotle and Cava, both of which seem to have a broader appeal.

Financials

Sweetgreen generated revenue of roughly $155 million in Q4 2025 which is a decrease of 3.5% compared to Q4 2024. For the quarter, same-store sales declined by 11.5%, whereas the company saw same-store sales increase by 4.4% in the prior year’s fourth quarter. Both restaurant-level profit and restaurant-level profit margin declined compared to the prior year, as Q4 2025 restaurant-level profit was $16.2 million and profit margins were 10.4%. In Q4 2024 restaurant-level profit was $28 million, and profit margins were 17.4%.

From an annual perspective, restaurant operating costs in 2025 outpaced the slight revenue growth. Additionally, pre-opening expenses and impairment and closure costs grew compared to 2024 which led to a wider loss from operations and net loss, as you can see below:

Sweetgreen investors continue to see more dilution as the number of weighted average shares outstanding has continued to increase over the last few years.

The company’s 2026 guidance wasn’t great, as management is expecting same-store sales to be down between 2% and 4%. Sweetgreen expects restaurant-level margin to be in the range of 14.2% to 14.7% and adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $1 to $6 million.

While a restaurant-level profit margin in the range of 14% is certainly better than 10%, it’s clear the company’s profit margins have been trending downward, as you can see below:

Sweetgreen must improve this metric if it’s going to rebound.

Sweetgreen has a decent balance sheet, as you can see below, with slightly more current assets than current liabilities. However, their accumulated deficit continues to grow, as you can see below:

Valuation

Sweetgreen has a valuation grade of a "C" at Seeking Alpha:

As Sweetgreen remains unprofitable, I believe price to sales is likely the best valuation metric for this business.

Sweetgreen has a higher price-to-sales ratio than the sector median; however, the company’s current P/S ratio (TTM) is much lower than their historic five-year average. Also, compared to other growth restaurant stocks like Cava Group, Inc., and Dutch Bros, Inc. (BROS), Sweetgreen's forward P/S ratio is much more reasonable:

However, Cava and Dutch Bros do have significantly better outlooks, I'd argue, as most analysts think those two companies can continue to deliver double-digit revenue growth over the next few years.

For Sweetgreen, analysts are expecting low- to mid-single-digit revenue growth over the next few years, which I think still may be the best-case scenario with how the overall macro picture is playing out and how consumers are turning to lower-priced alternatives. I find it difficult to believe the company’s theory consumers will be willing to pay higher prices for higher quality ingredients will hold water.

Conclusion

The struggles continue for Sweetgreen as restaurant profit margins are falling as the business is having trouble improving traffic trends. I’m not so sure operational efficiencies will improve such trends, and I’m not a believer that wraps will be the thing that brings back consumers in this economy where Sweetgreen’s bread-and-butter younger consumers certainly seem to be struggling.

Until profit margin and traffic trends improve, I’m out on Sweetgreen and would caution investors trying to catch a falling knife, as I don’t think a speedy recovery is in sight.

data.title: FOF: Blended Portfolio Proven To Yield Long-Term Results
data.description:
Overview

During periods of market volatility and uncertainty, income funds like the Cohen & Steers Closed-End Opportunity Fund (FOF) can serve as an efficient hedge against the downside. However, the debt markets have faced their own set of unique headwinds with interest rates remaining at the higher end of their historical range. The elevated cost of debt has created an elevated risk for some income funds due to the potential threat of defaults. However, I believe that FOF was dragged down alongside the rest of the debt markets, despite the fund's greater diversity in equity and commodity funds. When I previously covered FOF, I issued a buy rating due to the fund's resilience. Now that the environment has shifted a bit, I wanted to revisit the fund's overall value proposition through 2026.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FOF's share price has increased by more than 15.7%. Despite the fears around the health of the debt markets, FOF has been able to provide investors with capital appreciation. When including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return jumps up above 25.5% over the same time frame. Now that the share price has pulled back from its prior highs, FOF now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.6%, while issuing payouts on a monthly basis.

However, FOF's strategy goes beyond traditional debt investments. For this reason, I anticipate that FOF will hold up a lot better than debt-focused peers over the next few quarters. Despite this, I still believe that the fund's performance may be negatively impacted by sentiment, even if the actual positions within FOF remain solid. So valuation can be a bit tricky here because there is a possibility that FOF remains more resilient than peers, but there is also a chance that FOF's downside momentum continues if interest rates are not reduced.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, FOF now has total net assets of $366.6M that are spread across 108 different holdings. FOF is essentially a 'fund of funds' since it has exposure to a diverse range of other closed-end funds. This approach allows FOF to collect a high rate of income and provide shareholders with a high dividend yield. FOF is actively managed so the allocation between fund types can change based on market conditions. The fund's current allocation by asset class is as follows:

  • Equity funds: 49%
  • Commodity Funds: 23%
  • Fixed Income Funds: 19%
  • Municipal Funds: 6%
  • Cash: 3%

The allocation to equity funds allows FOF to participate in some capital appreciation as the indices expand over time. Therefore, FOF can get partial exposure to some of the growth of large-cap leaders within the technology sector. When looking at the top ten holdings, FOF is getting this equity exposure through positions like Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX), for example. ADC is a closed-end fund that provides direct equity exposure to the Mag 7 companies, including many other high quality equities.

Similarly, the inclusion of commodity funds has allowed FOF to also participate in the massive gold and silver rally that we've experienced over the last year. This was achieved through positions in funds like the Sprott Physical Gold and Silver Trust (CEF). Despite the fund leaning a bit top heavy in its top ten holdings, I don't think this is much of an issue. Most of the closed-end funds, with the exception of the commodity funds, provide instant diversification across hundreds of different positions within.

In fact, FOF's portfolio allocation to traditional debt securities makes up a smaller portion of total assets. According to the top ten holdings, the exposure to the debt markets is achieved through the various PIMCO and BlackRock funds, which may allocate a percentage of assets towards varying forms of debt securities, including high yield credit, corporate bonds, or mortgage-backed securities, just to name a few.

Performance & Macro Resilience

Since my last coverage, FOF has released an updated annual report for the 2025 period. This report provides us with transparency into the fund's actual earnings and may reveal any strengths or weaknesses FOF may have. Net investment income amounted to $0.35 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.92 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income slightly increased year-over-year, but was still a bit of a decline from the higher end of its historical range. Net investment income is generated from the combination of the dividends collected from equities and the interest collected from debt securities.

However, net investment income makes up a smaller portion of FOF's earnings. The fund's allocation to equity and commodity based positions has allowed FOF to capture a meaningful level of net realized gains. So when the positions within FOF's portfolio rises, the fund has to sell off these positions and use the proceeds to support the distributions. The efficiency of its strategy has proven to lead to NAV growth over the last few years, despite a changing interest rate environment. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $14.19 per share
  • 2022: $10.62 per share
  • 2023: $10.82 per share
  • 2024: $11.97 per share
  • 2025: $13.20 per share

This continued NAV growth has translated to FOF delivering consistent price appreciation over time. This has worked in the favor of investors and I believe that capital appreciation is most likely when the distribution policy isn't overly generous. This is why the valuation is a bit tricky for a fund like FOF. High quality funds tend to trade at a premium valuation when investors have a high level of confidence in the performance. With that said, FOF traded at a premium to NAV of 2.67% at the time of my last coverage. Following the recent pullback in share price, FOF now trades at a slightly smaller premium to NAV of 1.86%.

For reference, FOF has traded at an average premium to NAV of 1.7% over the last five years. FOF actually trades at a fairly reasonable price to NAV valuation relative to its historical range. I believe that FOF's recent pullback may be attributed to the recent pullback in gold price from its high in mid-January, as well as the overall sentiment of the debt markets. As interest rates remain elevated, this has increased the risk of borrower defaults in the market.

Despite several interest rate cuts initiated through 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. FOF does have exposure to debt securities with a credit rating below investment grade, so the fund is impacted by the sentiment of higher rates as well. However, FOF's diversity across other asset classes means that the risks associated with the risk of defaults may be overblown.

So with this in mind, I do believe that this remains an attractive time to accumulate shares. The fund has proven the efficiency of its strategy and active management style since inception, so I feel confident that management will be able to shift its allocation to different areas of the market over the next twelve months. For instance, I believe that FOF may reduce its exposure to commodity funds if we experience a scenario where the price of gold and silver retract.

Similarly, I trust that management will be able to increase its allocation to equity based funds if we see market indices head higher as the growth of AI continues. The only caveat is that investors should anticipate that most of the returns will come from the fund's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions going forward. So looking forward, I believe that FOF's structure makes it an efficient buy-and-hold fund for investors that want a high yield without the stress of worrying about changing market cycles, due to the diversity across different asset types.

Peer Comparison

There are a lot of high yield peers out there for investors to choose from. I order to demonstrate how FOF is truly unrivaled, it makes to sense run a performance comparison against these peers. While there underlying strategy may differ a bit, the emphasis on providing a high yield from a portfolio of mixed assets remains similar. I believe that the most notable high yield peers out there include funds such as:

  • John Hancock Premium Dividend Fund (PDT): ~7.45% dividend yield
  • Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS): ~7.95% dividend yield

As we can see, these peers offer very similar dividend yields and they all pay out distributions on a monthly frequency. PDT is the largest fund in terms of assets under management and it has the longest operating history with an inception dating back to 1989. PDT's strategy is a lot more straightforward than FOF's strategy. PDT allocates its investments to equity and preferred stocks to generate its high income. PDT also puts an emphasis on the Utilities and Financial sectors to take a defensive approach to its holdings.

On the other hand, CEFS is more closely aligned in its portfolio because it also operates as a 'fund of funds'. The main difference is that CEFS is an activist-led fund by Boaz Weinstein and their main goal is to identify funds that trade at a deep discount to NAV. CEFS is much more actively managed and they tend to take much larger positions in funds to help motivate a fund's management to reduce the discounted valuation. CEFS is a lot more focused on opportunities where they see an arbitrage opportunity, compared to FOF's buy-and-hold approach.

When comparing the total return of these peers over the last twelve months, we can see that FOF significantly outperforms. I believe the outperformance is attributed to the better portfolio diversity and less of an active management approach. CEFS's focus on trying to locate discounted opportunities hasn't led to compelling results through the current market environment. Similarly, PDT's lack of diversity outside of preferred securities means that its method for generating earnings isn't as flexible as FOF.

Risks

Since FOF operates as a fund of funds, the approach has some unique risks for investors. Not only do investors need to pay FOF's managed fee of 0.95% of assets, but there are a bunch of other management fees for the funds that FOF holds. While investors may not technically be responsible for these fees, it can result in 'acquired fund fees' that are higher than more traditional funds, such as PDT. In a scenario where FOF's earning decline and growth slows, these higher fees may actually chew into the overall total return potential of FOF.

Furthermore, the latest annual report reveals that the fund is more reliant on the net realized gains component to generate sufficient earning. If net realized gains fail to produce positive results, FOF may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it earns, which can lead to NAV erosion. Going forward, it would be ideal for net investment income levels to substantially improve so that the fund can offer better resilience through periods of market declines.

A good example of this vulnerability was demonstrated through 2022's market decline. Since the fund relies on net realized gains, market declines can threaten FOF's ability to lock in capital gains. As a result, the fund paid out more than it earned and this directly impacted the share price and NAV. So through the 2022 decline, FOF actually saw greater losses compared to CEFS and PDT over the same time frame.

Dividend Consistency

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.087 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.044 per share. What I like most about the fund is that it has demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that can support several years worth of payouts in a single period. For instance, the annual earnings for 2025 landed at $2.27 per share, which was enough to support more than two years worth of payouts. This excess income means that FOF can have a sort of cushion against years where the performance may be weak.

2025's earnings of $2.27 per share / $1.044 annual payout = 2.17 years.

Over the last five years, FOF has kept payouts very consistent and this makes it a great choice for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation. One of the reasons why distributions can remain so consistent is due to the structure that allows excess income to be generated and the realistic yield. I wouldn't anticipate any sort of dividend growth in the future because this paying out a greater portion of its earnings as a distribution may threaten the current strength of its operating spreads.

Furthermore, FOF's ability to keep payouts consistent has allowed long-term investors to create their own dividend growth. To demonstrate this, I ran a back test of an original $10,000 investment. This calculation assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $10K. It also assumes that all dividends were reinvested to acquire more shares over time. By simply remaining invested and reinvesting dividends, investors would have been able to roughly double their annual dividend income over the last ten years.

  • 2017 Dividend Income: $927
  • 2025 Dividend Income: $1,845

Lastly, FOF is a unique income position because the distributions may offer some tax-efficiency for investors. For instance, the most recently available section19(a) notice indicates that 80.40% of the payouts issued YTD were classified as net realized long-term capital gains, which are treated as qualified dividends and have the most favorable tax consequences for investors. Therefore, this provides some flexibility for FOF to be utilized outside of traditional tax-advantaged accounts.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that FOF is still a buy at its current price to NAV valuation. Despite the pullback in share price over the last month, FOF's diversified portfolio structure allows the fund to directly participate in the upside growth of equities and commodities, which can offset the decline of the debt markets. FOF's active allocation strategy also means that the fund is suited to continue navigating different market conditions, as long as management remains proactive. However, management has an excellent track record of generating earnings that exceed distributions, so I have confidence in FOF's potential as a long-term buy-and-hold position.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Still A Blue Chip, Just Not A Bargain
data.description:
EPD: Still A Blue Chip, Just Not A Bargain

I last covered Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) back in 2017, when the unit price was sitting around $25, and the MLP sector was still recovering from the 2015 oil crash. A lot has changed since then. The distribution has grown every year, the balance sheet has strengthened, and the stock has climbed to about $37. Investors who bought and held have done well.

But I'm not here to pat EPD on the back for its past results. I'm here because the stock has gotten ahead of itself, and right now, I think there are better places to put your money for midstream income, as I argue below.

Full-Year Numbers Were Impressive

First, I need to give credit where it's due. EPD's full-year 2025 was strong by any measure. Enterprise reported net income of $5.8 billion, distributable cash flow of $7.9 billion, and record Q4 adjusted EBITDA of $2.7 billion. The distribution came in at $2.175 per unit for the year, covered 1.7 times by DCF, making it more than safely covered. And this payout represents 28 consecutive years of distribution growth. Not many companies in any sector can say that.

The balance sheet is a fortress and another major benefit of owning this stock. An A-/A-/A3 credit rating, the highest in the midstream space. About $5.2 billion in total liquidity. Leverage at 3.3x with a target of roughly 3.0x by year-end 2026. Only about $35 billion in debt against an $80 billion market cap. And 98% of that debt is fixed-rate, with a weighted-average cost of 4.7%. This is not a company that keeps you up at night worrying about solvency.

Cash flow per unit has marched steadily higher for two decades, from about $1 per unit in 2006 to $3.98 in 2025. Through the financial crisis, the oil price collapse, and COVID, that number never stopped growing. It's a genuinely remarkable track record of operational durability.

On valuation, EPD isn't necessarily expensive. The forward P/E is about 13x, based on average analysts' estimates posted here at Seeking Alpha. The company repurchased roughly $300 million of units in 2025 under its $5 billion buyback program. Management is guiding for 3 to 5% growth in 2026 with a step-up to roughly 10% in 2027 as new projects reach fuller utilization. All good stuff.

So what's the problem?

The Yield Tells The Story

Here's the thing about MLPs: the distribution is the main event. It's the primary reason most investors buy these names, and it constitutes the majority of long-term total returns. When you look at EPD through that lens, it simply doesn't look as appealing as a stock.

EPD currently yields about 5.85%. Under 6% is the lowest yield this stock has carried since before COVID, when oil prices briefly went negative and every MLP was in crisis mode.

Look at the 10-year yield chart. Outside of the pandemic spike, EPD has typically traded with a yield between 6% and 8%. At 5.85%, you're buying at the most expensive valuation (by yield) in over six years.

Now run the math forward. If EPD continues growing the distribution at its five-year pace of roughly 4%, the payout could reach approximately $2.57 to $2.75 per unit by 2030. At today's $37 stock price, that implies a yield on cost of about 6.9% to 7.4%. That's a fine yield on cost, looking forward 4-5 years. But it's actually below where several of its peers yield today, before any growth. You're paying a premium for EPD's reliability, and that premium has gotten too rich.

Better Alternatives For Midstream Income

If you want midstream infrastructure exposure with a strong yield, here's what else is out there. All figures are accurate as of writing on March 12, and 5-year distribution growth numbers are estimates published by each company.

MPLX LP (MPLX) stands out to me (I own units, along with EPD, DMLP, and BSM). Similar toll-road business model, similar balance sheet quality, but it yields about 7.4% and has been growing distributions at roughly 9% annually over the past five years. That's more than double EPD's pace. Management has said they expect the current 12.5% annual distribution growth to continue for two more years. If you're going to own a pipeline MLP, MPLX gives you both a higher starting yield and faster growth.

Western Midstream Partners, LP (WES) is another strong option at nearly 9% yield and is another strong play, albeit it's a smaller company and carries a bit more risk than EPD.

For investors willing to accept more direct commodity sensitivity, Dorchester Minerals, L.P. (DMLP) and Black Stone Minerals, L.P. (BSM) offer royalty exposure at yields north of 8% and 10%, respectively. Those distributions are more variable because they're tied to production volumes and commodity prices. But the underlying economics are compelling, especially with zero debt on both balance sheets.

The Risks

Commodity price exposure is a key risk. Pipelines operate as toll-road businesses, so their cash flows are more about volume than price. But they're not completely immune to big price drops in oil and natural gas. A sustained decline in oil and gas production volumes, whether driven by a demand shock or regulatory changes, would hit throughput and eventually distribution. EPD's diversification across NGL, crude, natural gas, and petrochemical services mitigates this, but the correlation to commodity cycles is definitely a key risk to consider.

Interest rate sensitivity is another one. MLPs compete with bonds, REITs, and utilities for income-oriented capital. If rates stay higher for longer, yield-seeking investors may demand a wider spread from EPD, which means a lower unit price. The 98% fixed-rate debt insulates the company itself, but it doesn't insulate the stock.

Regulatory and energy transition risk hasn't really been discussed by investors lately, likely given the current administration's friendliness to oil and gas companies. But it's still considered a long-term risk factor. New pipeline construction faces increasing permitting hurdles, and a more aggressive shift toward electrification could reduce long-term hydrocarbon throughput. I think this risk is often overstated for companies like EPD with essential NGL and petrochemical exposure, but it's worth noting for a 20-year hold thesis.

Bottom Line: Hold

I want to be clear about something: Enterprise Products Partners is a great company. The track record speaks for itself. Twenty-seven years of distribution growth, an A-rated balance sheet, nearly $8 billion in annual distributable cash flow, and management that has navigated every commodity cycle without cutting the payout. If someone told me they wanted to buy EPD and hold it for 20 years, I wouldn't argue with them. That's why I own the stock here.

But great companies can be bad buys at the wrong price. At $37, you're getting the lowest yield EPD has offered since before COVID. You're paying a premium to peers that offer higher current income and, in MPLX's case, faster distribution growth. The projected distribution growth doesn't close the gap for years.

For me, a unit price under $32 would represent a solid entry point. That implies a yield of about 6.9%, bringing EPD back into its historical range and closer to peer yields. Under $30, though, and I'd be looking to load up. That's a yield north of 7.3%, and at that price, you're being properly compensated for what is admittedly one of the safest names in the sector.

Until then? This is a great company trading at a not-so-great price, so I recommend it as a Hold.

If you can break down the basic concept of what happens in the market dynamics when something like an earnings announcement hits and we see that pop in the price, why is that?

SC: Okay. So, typically getting you familiar with the concept of shorting, hedge funds will typically short stocks, and what they do is, they borrow shares from somebody who's long the stock. And when they borrow their shares, they sell them immediately. That process is called shorting, and, that reflects the short interest. So, as an example, if you look at the first stock here, Lumentum Holdings, and we go to the short interest column, you could see that 15% of the shares are short. So, that's fairly high.

If we go down even further to EZCORP, we could see 20% of the shares outstanding are short. So, that means that hedge funds have borrowed as much as 20%, and to be precise, 21.62% of the stock they've borrowed it, and they have short the shares. Now, if the stock goes down, that works well for the hedge fund because at the point that they initially borrow the shares, they sell it immediately. So, it's still the same concept in essence of buying low and selling high. They're selling it high, and then they hope to Buy it back at a lower price.

But the short squeeze is a situation where actually the company does well, and the share price does not go down, it actually starts to go up. And if you're a hedge fund and you're short the shares and it starts going up, it puts them in a difficult position. And what will happen is it's hard to cover their position. It usually takes a couple of days with many stocks. Once they even have a smaller cap, it could go up potentially days and days before they can cover the shares that they're short. And that is what we refer to as the short squeeze. When they're in a position and they can't cover the shares that they've borrowed, and they have to buy it back, that's known as the squeeze. And, boy, is that painful if you're a hedge fund? So, if you're a hedge fund and you own GameStop, you are crying or you're probably losing your job if you were short that stock.

Could end up being the same with many of these names because as we've highlighted, the fundamentals, most of the valuation frameworks are quite attractive. data.title: ATI: Aerospace Supercycle Meets Material Scarcity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

First and foremost, I believe that ATI Inc. (ATI) sits at the intersection of two things that rarely show up together in markets. Structural demand growth and constrained supply. And usually, when these two things collide, then pricing power tends to follow. And pricing power, especially in the material business, usually shows up directly in margins and cash flow.

ATI produces high-performance alloys and specialty metals that are used in aerospace engines, defense systems, and energy infrastructure. Of course, those are not optional end markets. Jet engines require heat-resistant alloys that can survive extreme conditions. On the other side, defense programs need materials that meet strict performance standards. And these components cannot simply be substituted with cheaper materials. Not to mention that certification cycles are quite long and lengthy. And once a supplier is qualified, then switching becomes quite painful and expensive.

Now, layer the aerospace cycle on top of that reality. Global aircraft production is booming, especially after the Covid pandemic. According to McKinsey & Company, there is a $1.8 trillion space economy growth. Space applications are expected to grow at a faster rate than global nominal GDP over the next decade. Already, according to the BBC, US defense spending is expected to increase to $1.5Tn. This will be a 50% increase from the approved budget that the US Congress had in December 2025. Of course, given the US-Israel-Iran war in the Middle East, President Trump noted that the US defense department needed a higher budget to build a dream military.

...This will allow us to build the 'Dream Military' that we have long been entitled to and, more importantly, that will keep us SAFE and SECURE, regardless of foe...

Also, there is very little exposure on the electrification trends currently happening in the aviation industry. This is mainly to reduce noise and air pollution. This is according to Lithium Energy. As the aviation sector transitions from the traditional jet engines to electric batteries, there is a need for ATI’s super alloys. And I would say this is cemented. ATI is expected to reach $5 Bn in revenue by 2027, driven largely by its reclassification as a dedicated Aerospace & Defense supplier (66% of Q1 2025 sales), placing it at the centre of the electrification "super cycle." It is also important to mention that post-pandemic, Airbus (EADSF) and Boeing (BA) both carry multi-year order backlogs. And as a result, they are increasing output to meet demand for new aircraft and replacement parts. And this is where it gets interesting because ATI sits directly in that supply chain. As aircraft production climbs, so does the demand for high-temperature alloys and titanium products.

I would also like to mention that the market is beginning to appreciate geopolitics. I say this because conflicts in the Middle East and the broader rise in global security tensions are pushing governments to increase defense spending. As of writing this, the US had announced that they would increase their defense budget by 50% to $1.5Tn. Also, other European and several NATO partners have already announced multiyear increases in military budgets. And this is because defense platforms rely heavily on high-performance materials. Jet engines, missile systems, armored vehicles, and naval reactors require alloys that are capable of operating in extreme temperatures and stress conditions. And this is where ATI sits, right? Because it is right in the middle of that supply chain. And this is why I am rating the company a Strong Buy.

Business Overview

ATI is a leading producer of specialty materials and components and has a market cap of $21.7B with strong liquidity. The company is headquartered in Dallas, Texas. Its main market is the defense, aerospace, and energy sectors. ATI holds a significant position in nickel superalloys used in next-generation jet engine hot sections and other metals that have extremely high barriers of entry, such as titanium. ATI is producing six of the seven most advanced jet engine nickel alloys. Yet the remaining alloys are produced by an OEM. It is also the sole supplier for 5 of 7 alloys used in the hot section, under long-term contracts that extend well into the 2030s and even into the 2040s. These two claims taken together position the company at a significant position inside the very specific high-value subset of nickel superalloys rather than the entire global nickel alloy market. It completely transformed into an aerospace and defense supplier, which secured its profits and allowed it to soar to new heights. In very simple terms, ATI sells high-performance materials that allow complex machines like jet engines and turbines to operate safely and efficiently.

ATI highlighted 2025 revenue of $4.6Bn, adjusted EBITDA of $859Mn and FCFs of $380Mn. I note that management pointed out that jet engines and defense were the core drivers. As I mentioned, specialty energy is also becoming another leg of growth for ATI. In my opinion, ATI is moving from being a beneficiary of demand recovery to being a company with structural earnings power. And that usually deserves a better multiple than standard metal names, as I will discuss in the valuation section.

Now that I have talked about why

Growth Drivers

Most investors still view ATI as a steel and metals company, but the business has significantly evolved. I will be discussing some of the underappreciated drivers that I believe are the key to my investment thesis.

First, ATI produces six of the seven most advanced nickel- and cobalt-based alloys and superalloys used in jet engines. These engines operate at temperatures above 1000°C, requiring specialized alloys capable of maintaining structural integrity under extreme conditions. Lead times for these alloys have doubled in a single quarter, and once qualified in a jet engine program, suppliers are extremely difficult to replace, as I mentioned earlier. Also, engine makers like GE (GE) and Pratt & Whitney face 2-3 year backlogs for repairs. Therefore, ATI’s forgings and castings capture more than 70% margins on high-demand titanium & nickel parts. And this enables the company to move faster than original equipment manufacturers by fixing issues left behind. At the end of the day, all this gives ATI an embedded pricing power.

Then secondly, airlines are replacing older aircraft with next-generation models to reduce fuel consumption, emissions, and maintenance costs. Examples include replacing Airbus A320ceo with newer A320neo aircraft and Boeing 737 Next Generation with 737 MAX aircraft. These newer engines require more ATI materials, thereby increasing ATI's revenue per aircraft. Not forgetting that the aviation sector is being electrified, and ATI’s niobium alloys enable next-gen electric aircraft motors and EV power electronics.

The third driver is that jet engine sales grew 21% y/y in 2025. And defense revenue increased 14% in 2025. Additionally, missile-related sales increased 127% y/y. So, why is this relevant? Because, as I mentioned earlier, ATI supplies key materials like C103 Niobium Alloy and Titanium alloys that are used in rocket nozzles, hypersonic vehicles, and precision-guided missile systems.

U.S. budgets under President Trump prioritize hypersonic munitions. The plan includes $3.9 Bn for hypersonic weapons and $6.5 Bn for conventional munitions, alongside a Golden Dome missile defense system. ATI’s specialty metals and components are powering approximately 20% of the F-35 Lightning II stealth strike fighter and various missile programs, with contracts accelerating amid geopolitical tensions.

Lastly, it is important to mention that growth is coming from three compounding drivers that line up with ATI's mix and contract structure. First, I would say it is the next-gen engine ramps. This is something that management highlighted: that ATI's isothermal forging deliveries to Pratt & Whitney grew 6x. Secondly, there is defense, where the company expects mid-teens growth and advancement in missile programs like PAC-3 and THAAD. Thirdly is specialty energy. Renewed long-term contracts expanded ATI's share by more than 20% and positioned ATI as a majority supplier with demand supported by nuclear and gas turbine markets.

Now, with all these in mind, it’s easy to see that ATI is positioned for a sustained earnings expansion mainly because of its expanding margins, strong FCF growth, and increasing exposure to long-term aerospace programs. The market, however, underestimates the long-term earnings power created by the global aerospace venture, rising defense spending, and ATI’s shift toward higher-margin aerospace and defense materials; that’s why ATI remains undervalued, although recent operating performance demonstrates accelerating growth and improving profitability.

Valuation

As of writing this, ATI was trading at $145 per share. And despite its strong growth, ATI trades at a discount to aerospace peers such as Howmet Aerospace (HWM) and Carpenter Technology Corp. (CRS).

(ATI) (HWM) (CRS)
FWD Non-GAAP PEG 1.56x 2.12x 1.59x
FWD P/E 37x 54x 39x
FWD EV/EBITDA 22x 36x 24x
FWD EV/FCF 26x 47x 34x
ROE 24.62% 29.98% 23.49%
Debt/Equity 1.04x 0.68x 0.35x

As you can see, ATI trades at a lower FWD Non-GAAP PEG compared to HWM and CRS. Additionally, I also note that it has a lower multiple when it comes to FWD P/E and FWD EV/EBITDA. ATI's PEG of 1.56x is trading at a 7% discount compared to the sector median, which trades at 1.68x. It is also important to mention that ATI scores a decent A- growth grade.

ATI's Key Risk

ATI still faces a few risks that could affect my thesis. The biggest risk I see is delays in aircraft production, which could slow the demand for its materials. This is in as much as long-term supply agreements and steady aftermarket demand provide some stability. The other risk is that commodity price swings, especially in nickel and titanium, can also pressure margins. Although ATI typically manages this through supply contracts and pricing mechanisms, it is still a risk that could definitely affect future revenues for ATI. The U.S.-Iran war and geopolitical tensions, including the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, are supporting defense spending. But a sudden end to these conflicts could reduce the urgency of military expenditure and weaken demand for some of ATI’s products, which is a big risk.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I believe ATI warrants a Strong Buy rating. As I have demonstrated, ATI trades at a discount relative to the sector median FWD Non-PEG. And this shows me that the market still hasn’t fully caught up with ATI’s improving profitability and earnings trajectory. At the same time, the company benefits from a more structurally protected product portfolio in the aerospace and defense sectors. This protects them from harsh competition, as there are strict qualification requirements that a lot of companies can’t deal with and long-term supply relationships that limit clients. Together, these factors create a clear valuation gap that shows an opportunity as the market begins to recognize ATI’s true earning potential. And for these reasons, I am rating ATI a Strong Buy.
